questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A circle having centre at $C$ is made to pass through the point $P(1,2)$, touching the straight lines $7 x-y=5$ and $x+y+13=0$ at $A$ and $B$ respectively, then
options: area of quadrilateral ACBP is 100 sq. units
area of quadriateral ACBP is 200 sq. units
questions.solution: Clearly, the point lies on $7 x-y=5$ Also, centre of the circle must lie on the bisectors of the lines $x+y+13=0$ and $7 x-y-5=0$ given by $\frac{x+y+13}{\sqrt{2}}= \pm \frac{7 x-y-5}{\sqrt{50}} \Rightarrow x-3 y=35 \text { and } 3 x+y+15=0$ Let $(h, k)$ be the centre of the circle, then $h-3 k=35 \ldots \ldots$(1) and $3 \mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{k}=15 \ldots \ldots$(2) Clearly $C B$ is perpendicular $B P \Rightarrow \frac{k-2}{h-1} \times 7=-1 \Rightarrow h+7 k-15=0 \ldots \ldots$(3) On solving, we get centres as $C_1 \equiv(29-2)$ and $C_2 \equiv(-6,3)$ $\Rightarrow r_1^2=800$ and $r_1^2=50 \Rightarrow$ smaller circle has radius $=\sqrt{50}$ Therefore area of quadrilateral ACBP $=2\left[\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{50} \times \sqrt{200}\right]$ sq. units
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Consider the circle $x^2+y^2-10 x-6 y+30=0$. Let $O$ be the centre of the circle and tangents at $A(7,3)$ and $B(5,1)$ meet at $C$. Let $S=0$ represents family of circles passing through $A$ and $B$, then -
options: the radical axis for the family of circle $S=0$ is $x+y=10$
the smallest possible circle of the family $S=0$ is $x^2+y^2-12 x-4 y+38=0$
questions.solution:
$(x-5)^2+(y-3)^2=4$
Option $(\mathrm{A}): \mathrm{A}(\square \mathrm{OACB})=4$ Option (B) : $S \equiv\left(x^2+y^2-10 x-6 y+30\right)+\lambda(2-y-4)=0$ Obviously radical axis is $x-y=4$ Option (B) is incorrect Option $(C):(x-5)(x-7)+(y-1)(y-3)=0$ \{The required circles diameter is AB \} $x^2+y^2-12 x-4 y+38=0$ Option (C) is correct Option (D) is obviously correct
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Tangents are drawn to the circle $x^2+y^2=50$ from a point ' $P$ ' lying on the $x$-axis. These tangents meet the $y$-axis at points ' $P_1$ ' and ' $\mathrm{P}_2$ '. Possible coordinates of ' $P$ ' so that area of triangle $P P_1 P_2$ is minimum, is/are
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{OP}=5 \sqrt{2} \sec \theta \\ & \mathrm{OP}_1=5 \sqrt{2} \operatorname{cosec} \theta\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Delta P P_1 P_2=\frac{100}{\sin 2 \theta} \\ & \left(\Delta \mid P P_1 P_2\right)_{\min }=100 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\pi / 4 \\ & \Rightarrow O P=10 \\ & \Rightarrow P=(10,0),(-10,0)\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $a$ and $b$ be two real numbers such that $a^2-3 b^2+4 a+1=0$. If the line $a x+b y+1=0$ touches a fixed circle $\forall A$ and $b$, then which of the following is/are correct?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 4 a^2+4 a+1=3 b^2+3 a^2 \\
& \frac{(2 a+0 \cdot b+1)^2}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}=\sqrt{3}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Centre of the circle is $(2,0)$, radius is $\sqrt{3}$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The circles $x^2+y^2-4 x-81=0, x^2+y^2+24 x-81=0$ intersect each other at points $A$ and $B$. $A$ line through point $A$ meet one circle at $P$ and a parallel line through $B$ meet the other circle at $Q$. Then the locus of the mid point of $P Q$ is
questions.solution: Now let $P\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ and $Q\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ Now solve $S_1$ and PA then $x_1=\frac{-18 m-24}{m^2+1}$ and $y_1=\frac{-9 m^2-24 m+9}{m^2+1}$ Now solve $S_2$ and $B Q$ then $x_2=\frac{18 m+4}{m^2+1}$ and $y_2=\frac{9 m^2+4 m-9}{m^2+1}$ Let mid pt of $P Q$ is $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ then $\mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{x}_2=2 \mathrm{~h}$ and $\mathrm{y}_1+\mathrm{y}_2=2 \mathrm{k}$ $x_1+x_2=\frac{-20}{m^2+1}=2 h$ $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}}$ $y_1+y_2=\frac{-20 m}{m^2+1}=2 k \Rightarrow \frac{-20}{\left(\frac{k}{h}\right)^2+1}=2 h$ $h\left(h^2+k^2+10 h\right)=0$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The centre of a circle $S=0$ lies on $2 x-2 y+9=0$ and $S=0$ cuts orthogonally the circle $x^2+y^2=4$. Then the circle must pass through the point
questions.solution:
Let $S \equiv x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$
$\because \quad$ it cuts $x^2+y^2=4$ orthogonally
$\Rightarrow \quad c=4$
Moreover $\quad-2 g+2 f+9=0$
$(\because(-g,-f)$ satisfy the given equation)
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & S \equiv x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+4=0 \\
\Rightarrow & x^2+y^2+(2 f+9) x+2 f y+4=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \left(x^2+y^2+9 x+4\right)+2 f(x+y)=0
\end{array}$

It is of the form $S+\lambda P=0$ and hence passes through the intersection of $S=0$ and $P=0$ which when solved give $(-1 / 2$, $1 / 2),(-4,4)$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $(a, 0)$ is a point on a diameter of the circle $x^2+y^2=4$, then the equation $x^2-6 x-a^2=0$ has -
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=x^2-6 x-a^2$ and as $(a, 0)$ lies on the diameter of the circle so maximum value of $\mathrm{a}^2$ can be 4 Now $\begin{array}{ll} & f(-1)=1+6-\mathrm{a}^2\gt0 \\ & f(0)=-\mathrm{a}^2 \lt 0 \\ & f(3)=9-18-\mathrm{a}^2 \lt 0 \\ & f(7)=49-42-\mathrm{a}^2\gt0\end{array}$
so the equation $x^2-6 x-a^2=0$ has one root between -1 and $0 \&$ one root betwen 3 and 7.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: For the circles $x^2+y^2-10 x+16 y+89-r^2=0$ and $x^2+y^2+6 x-14 y+42=0$ which of the following is/are true.
options: Number of integral values of r are 7 for which circles are intersecting.
Number of integral values of r are 9 for which circles are intersecting.
For r equal to 13 number of common tangents are 3.
For r equal to 21 number of common tangents are 2.
questions.solution:
We have $(x-5)^2+(y+8)^2=25+64+r^2-89$ and $(x+3)^2+(y-7)^2=49+9-42=16$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(x-5)^2+(y+8)^2=r^2 \\ & \text { and }(x+3)^2+(y-7)^2=(4)^2\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow=\sqrt{289}=17=$ distance between their centres $\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \quad|r-4| \lt 17 \lt r+4 \\ & \Rightarrow r+4\gt17 \\ & \Rightarrow r\gt13\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { and }-17 \lt r-4 \lt 17 \\ & \Rightarrow-13 \lt r \lt 21 \\ & \text { Hence, } 13 \lt r \lt 21\end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad$ Possible values of 'r' can be $14,15,16,17,18,19,20$ Hence required sum = 119
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ and $\frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{d}=1$ where $a, b, c, d>0$ intersect the axes at four con-cyclic points and $a^2+c^2=b^2+d^2$, then the lines can intersect at which of the following given points?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & a \cdot c=b \cdot d \quad \text { [power of point] } \\ & a^2+c^2=b^2+d^2\end{aligned}$ $(a-c)^2=(b-d)^2 \quad$ and $\quad(a+c)^2=(b+d)^2$ $\therefore \quad a+c=b+d \quad$ and $\quad a-c=b-d$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad a=b \quad \text { and } \quad c=d \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text { Both line have slope }=-1 \Rightarrow \text { Parallel lines never meet }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad a-c=a-b \\ & a=d \quad \text { and } \quad b=c\end{aligned}$ $\frac{x}{d}+\frac{y}{c}=1 ; \quad \frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{d}=1$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad c x+d y=d x+c y \quad \Rightarrow \quad c(x-y)=d(x-y) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=x \\ & 2 \text { lines meet } y=x .\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $S: x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$ intersects both the lines $x y-3 x=0$ orthogonally and touches the circle $x^2+y^2-6 x-12 y+36=0$ externally, then:
options: radius of the circle $S$ lies in $(1,2)$
radius of the circle $S$ lies in $(2,3)$
equation of the transverse common tangent to both the circle is $x+y-3 \sqrt{2}-9=0$
questions.solution: $x(y-3)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad(0,3)$ is the centre $\begin{aligned} & c_1 c_2=r_1+r_2 \\ & \sqrt{9+9}=r+3 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & r=3(\sqrt{2}-1) \simeq 1.2\end{aligned}$ Transverse common tangent to both the circles $x+y+k=0$ $\begin{gathered}\text { applying } p=r \Rightarrow k= \pm 3 \sqrt{2}-9 \\ x+y+3 \sqrt{2}-9=0 \quad \text { or } \\ x+y-3 \sqrt{2}-9=0 \quad \text { (rejected) }\end{gathered}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $S$ be a circle with centre $O$ and radius 2. $A$ and $B$ be two points on the circle. $\angle A O B=x$, tangents at $A$ and $B$ intersect at $D$ and $O A$ and $B D$ intersect at $C$. Then which of the following must be correct?
options: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle O B C)}{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle O A B)}=1$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text { Area }(\triangle O B C)}{\text { Area }(\triangle O A B)}=2$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text { Area }(\triangle A D B)}{(\text { Area }(\triangle O A B))^3}=\frac{1}{16}$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle A D B)}{(\operatorname{Area}(\triangle O A B))^3}=\frac{1}{4}$
questions.solution: $r=2$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad A D=r \tan \frac{x}{2}=B D \\ & \text { Area }(\triangle O B C)=\frac{1}{2} r^2 \tan x \text { and Area }(\triangle O A B)=\frac{1}{2} r^2 \sin x \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle O B C)}{\text { Area }(\triangle O A B)}=1 \\ & \text { Area }(\triangle A D B)=\frac{1}{2} \cdot A D \cdot B D \sin (\pi-x)=\frac{1}{2} r^2 \tan ^2 \frac{x}{2} \sin x \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text { Area }(\triangle A B D)}{(\text { Area }(\triangle O A B))^3}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{1}{2} r^2 \tan ^2 \frac{x}{2} \sin x}{\left(\frac{1}{2} r^2 \sin x\right)^3} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{4 r^2 \tan ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{r^6 \sin ^2 x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{4 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{16 \cdot 4 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2} \cdot \cos ^4 \frac{x}{2}}=\frac{1}{16}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: From the point of intersection of the circle $S: x^2+y^2-4 x+6 y+13=0$ and the line $L: 2 x+5 y+11=0$ two tangents are drawn to the circle $\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2=\frac{121}{29}$, whose slopes are $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$, then -
questions.solution:
$S:(x-2)^2+(y+3)^2=0$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{S}$ is a point circle which represents point $(2,-3)$ and this point $(2,-3)$ also lies on the line
$L: 2 x+5 y+11=0$
Equation of tangents from $(2,-3)$ to the circle
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2=\frac{121}{29} \text { is } \mathrm{y}+3=\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{x}-2) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{mx}-\mathrm{y}=3+2 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
Applying $\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{r}$
$\begin{aligned} & \left|\frac{3+2 \mathrm{~m}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{m}^2}}\right|=\sqrt{\frac{121}{29}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 29\left(9+12 \mathrm{~m}+4 \mathrm{~m}^2\right)=121\left(1+\mathrm{m}^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 \mathrm{~m}^2-348 \mathrm{~m}-140=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{348}{5}\end{aligned}$
$\& \quad m_1 m_2=-\frac{140}{5}=-28$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Consider the circles $S_1: x^2+y^2=4$ and $S_2: x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y+4=0$ which of the following statements are correct?
options: Number of common tangents to these circles is 2.
If the power of a variable point P w.r.t. these two circles is same then P moves on the line $x+2 y-4=0$
Sum of the $y$-intercepts of both the circles is 6.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & S_1: x^2+y^2=4 \text { and } S_2=x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y+4=0 \\ & \text { centre: }(0,0) ; \text { radius }=2 \text { centre }:(1,2) ; \text { radius }=1\end{aligned}$
(A) $\mathrm{d}=$ distance between centres $=\sqrt{5}$ $r_1+r_2=3$
$\quad$ $r_1+r_2=3$
$\Rightarrow\left|r_1-r_2\right|=1$
$\therefore\left|r_1-r_2\right| \lt d \lt r_1+r_2$
$\therefore \quad$ these 2 circles are intersecting.
$\therefore \quad$ number of common tangents is 2 .
(B) $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ power of point P is same w.r.t. these two circles.
$\therefore \sqrt{h^2+k^2-4}=\sqrt{h^2+k^2-2 h-4 k+4}-4=-2 h-4 k+4$
$\begin{aligned} & 2 h+4 k-8=0 \\ & x+2 y-4=0\end{aligned}$
(C) $y$ intercept of $S_1$ is $2 \sqrt{+4}=4$ 
$\quad$ $y$ intercept of $\mathrm{S}_2$ is $2 \sqrt{4-4}=0$
$\therefore \quad$ sum of y -intercept $=4$
(D) $2(0+0)=-4+4$
$\therefore \quad$ circle is orthogonal.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: $P$ is a point on the parabola $y^2=4 x$ where abscissa and ordinate are equal. Equation of a circle passing through the focus and touching the parabola at $P$ is:
questions.solution:
$a t^2=2 a t$ point
$\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{t}=2,(0,0), \quad(4,4)$ (I) when $P \equiv(0,0)$ $\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2+\lambda(\mathrm{x})=0$ pass the $(1,0)$ $\lambda=-1$, equation tagent al $(0,0)$ $\mathrm{y}^2=4 \mathrm{x}$ Equ. $x^2+y^2-x=0$ $\begin{aligned} & y \cdot y_1=2\left(x+x_1\right) \\ & x=0 \end{aligned}$ (II) when point $(4,4), 2 x-2 y+8=0$ $(x-4)^2+(y-4)^2+\mu(2 x-2 y+8)=0$ pass $(1,0)$ Equation $x^2+y^2-13 x+2 y+12=0$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: $x^2+y^2=a^2$ and $(x-2 a)^2+y^2=a^2$ are two equal circles touching each other. Find the equation of circle (or circles) of the same radius touching both the circles.
questions.solution:
Given circles are
$x^2+y^2=a^2$ $\ldots(1)$
and $(x-2 a)^2+y^2=a^2$ $\ldots(2)$
Let $A$ and $B$ be the centres and $r_1$ and $r_2$ the radii of the circles (1) and (2) respectively. Then $A \equiv(0,0), B \equiv(2 a, 0), r_1=a, r_2=a$
Now $A B=\sqrt{(0-2 a)^2+0^2}=2 a=r_1+r_2$
Hence the two circles touch each other extenally.
Let the equation of the circle having same radius 'a' and touching the circles (1) and (2) be
$(x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=a^2$ $\ldots(3)$
Its centre $C$ is $(\alpha, \beta)$ and radius $r_3=a$
Since circle (3) touches the circle (1),
$A C=r_1+r_3=2 a \cdot\left[H e r e A C \neq\left|r_1-r_3\right|\right.$ as $\left.r_1-r_3=a-a=0\right]$
$\Rightarrow A C^2=4 a^2 \Rightarrow \alpha^2+\beta^2=4 a^2$ $\ldots(4)$
Again since circle (3) touches the circle (2)
$\begin{aligned}& B C=r_2+r_3 \Rightarrow B C^2=\left(r_2\right) \\& \Rightarrow(2 a-\alpha)^2+\beta^2=(a+a)^2 \Rightarrow \alpha^2+\beta^2-4 a \alpha=0 \\& \Rightarrow 4 a^2-4 a \alpha=0[\text { from (4)] }\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=\mathrm{a}$ and from (4), we have $\beta= \pm \sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}$.
Hence, the required circles are
$(x-a)^2+(y \mp a \sqrt{3})^2=a^2$
or $x^2+y^2-2 a x \mp 2 \sqrt{3} a y+3 a^2=0$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Consider two circles $C_1: x^2+y^2-1=0$ and $C_2: x^2+y^2-2=0$. Let $A(1,0)$ be a fixed point on the circle $C_1$ and $B$ be any variable point on the circle $C_2$. The line BA meets the curve $C_2$ again at $C$. Which of the following alternative(s) is/are correct?
options: Locus of midpoint of AB is a circle of radius $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
Locus of midpoint of $A B$ is a circle of area $\frac{\pi}{2}$
questions.solution:
We have maximum $\mathrm{BC}=2 \sqrt{2}$ and minimum $\mathrm{BC}=2$ $\therefore \mathrm{OA}^2+\mathrm{OB}^2+\mathrm{BC}^2 \in[7,11]$ Let $M$ be the midpoint of $A B$. $\Rightarrow M \equiv\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{2} \cos \theta}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}{2}\right)=(h, k)$
$\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{k}}{\sqrt{2}}, \cos \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}-1}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\therefore \quad$ Now on squaring \& adding, we get $\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{k}^2+(2 \mathrm{~h}-1)^2=2$ $\therefore \quad$ Locus of $M(h, k)$ is $\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+y^2=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
